title: Warhammer, Chaos in the Old World

walkthrough.title: Place the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Chaos in the Old World set up. First, place the game board in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. If this is your first time playing, you will need to assemble the four Threat dials onto the board using the plastic connectors. Push one half of each connector pair through the board and the other half through the dial, then snap them together. Make sure each dial is in the correct position: Khorne on top, Nurgle to the right, Tzeentch on the bottom, and Slaanesh to the left. Once attached, the dials stay on permanently. Set each dial so the word Start is visible in the main window, just above the three o'clock position.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Power Sheets and Arrange Seating
walkthrough.text: Now hand out the four Power sheets, one per player. Each sheet corresponds to a Chaos god: Khorne the Blood God, Nurgle the Plaguelord, Tzeentch the Changer of Ways, and Slaanesh the Prince of Pleasure. You can assign them by consensus or randomly. If you have only three players, return the unused Power sheet and all of its matching components to the box. Once everyone has their Power sheet, arrange seating in clockwise order: Khorne first, then Nurgle to Khorne's left, then Tzeentch, then Slaanesh. Skip over any powers not in play.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Each Power's Components
walkthrough.text: Each player takes all the components matching their Chaos Power. That includes: one power marker (for tracking power points on your sheet), one victory point marker (for the VP track on the board), 29 corruption tokens in your color, your deck of 24 Chaos cards, your 5 upgrade cards, and all of your plastic follower figures. Khorne gets 1 Bloodthirster, 6 Bloodletters, and 4 Bloodsworn. Nurgle gets 1 Great Unclean One, 5 Plaguebearers, and 6 Lepers. Tzeentch gets 1 Lord of Change, 3 Horrors, and 8 Acolytes. Slaanesh gets 1 Keeper of Secrets, 3 Daemonettes, and 6 Seductresses. Tuck your upgrade cards partway under one edge of your Power sheet so everyone can see which upgrades are not yet in play.

walkthrough.title: Place Power Markers and Victory Point Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player places their power marker on the highlighted starting space of the power point track on their Power sheet. This is the number of power points you will have each round to summon figures and play cards. Then place your victory point marker on the 0 space of the victory point track on the game board. Everyone starts at zero.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Common Tokens, Dice, and Ruination Cards
walkthrough.text: Separate all the dial advancement counters and Old World tokens into piles by type and place them near the board where everyone can reach them. The Old World tokens include 6 Event tokens, 4 Hero tokens, 6 Noble tokens, 20 Peasant tokens, 6 Skaven tokens, and 14 Warpstone tokens. Place the 5 battle dice near the board as well. Finally, sort the 5 ruination cards into a face-down stack ordered by their number, with the number 1 card on top and number 5 on the bottom. Place this stack on the game board in the indicated space.

walkthrough.title: Create the Old World Deck
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all 28 Old World cards together. Then deal cards face down from the top of the shuffled stack to form the Old World deck. For a four-player game, deal 7 cards. For a three-player game, deal 8 cards. Place this deck face down on the board in the Old World deck space. Return the remaining Old World cards to the box without looking at them. They will not be used this game.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Old World Tokens
walkthrough.text: Separate out 2 Noble tokens, 3 Warpstone tokens, and 4 Peasant tokens from the stockpiles. That is 9 tokens total. Mix them together face down so no one knows which is which. Then randomly draw tokens one at a time and place each on the board, one per map region, in the standard region order: Norsca, Troll Country, Kislev, The Empire, Bretonnia, Estalia, Tilea, The Border Princes, The Badlands. When you are done, every region has exactly one Old World token.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Draw Chaos Cards
walkthrough.text: Each player shuffles their own 24 Chaos cards to form a personal Chaos deck, then draws a starting hand of 3 cards. Place the remaining cards face down near your Power sheet. Keep your hand hidden from other players. There is no hand size limit in this game, so you will draw more as the game goes on. Once everyone has their starting hand, you are ready to begin the first round with the Old World phase.

summary.title: Place the Game Board
bullets: Place board centrally, assemble 4 Threat dials with plastic connectors (first game only)
Dial positions: Khorne top, Nurgle right, Tzeentch bottom, Slaanesh left
Set each dial so 'Start' text is visible in the main window

summary.title: Distribute Power Sheets and Arrange Seating
bullets: Give each player one Power sheet (Khorne, Nurgle, Tzeentch, or Slaanesh)
Assign by consensus or randomly; return unused sheet to box if 3 players
Seat clockwise: Khorne, Nurgle, Tzeentch, Slaanesh (skip absent powers)

summary.title: Distribute Each Power's Components
bullets: Each player gets: 1 power marker, 1 VP marker, 29 corruption tokens, 24 Chaos cards, 5 upgrade cards, and all plastic followers
Khorne: 1 Bloodthirster + 6 Bloodletters + 4 Bloodsworn (11 figures, red)
Nurgle: 1 Great Unclean One + 5 Plaguebearers + 6 Lepers (12 figures, green)
Tzeentch: 1 Lord of Change + 3 Horrors + 8 Acolytes (12 figures, blue)
Slaanesh: 1 Keeper of Secrets + 3 Daemonettes + 6 Seductresses (10 figures, purple)

summary.title: Place Power Markers and Victory Point Markers
bullets: Place power marker on highlighted starting space of your Power sheet's track
Place VP marker on the '0' space of the board's victory point track

summary.title: Prepare Common Tokens, Dice, and Ruination Cards
bullets: Sort Old World tokens into piles: 6 Event, 4 Hero, 6 Noble, 20 Peasant, 6 Skaven, 14 Warpstone
Place 16 dial advancement counters and 5 battle dice near the board
Stack 5 ruination cards in order (1 on top, 5 on bottom) on the board

summary.title: Create the Old World Deck
bullets: Shuffle all 28 Old World cards together
Deal face-down: 7 cards (4 players) or 8 cards (3 players)
Place deck on board; return unused cards to box unseen

summary.title: Place Starting Old World Tokens
bullets: Mix 2 Nobles + 3 Warpstone + 4 Peasants = 9 tokens total
Randomly place 1 token per region in standard region order (Norsca through Badlands)
Every region starts with exactly 1 Old World token

summary.title: Shuffle and Draw Chaos Cards
bullets: Each player shuffles their own 24-card Chaos deck
Draw a starting hand of 3 cards (no hand size limit)
Begin the first round with the Old World phase

walkthrough.title: Game Structure and Victory Conditions
walkthrough.text: Chaos in the Old World is played over a series of rounds. Each round has six phases in order: Old World Phase, Draw Phase, Summoning Phase, Battle Phase, Corruption Phase, and End Phase. The game can end in four ways, checked in this order at the end of each round. First, if any player's Threat dial reaches Victory, that player wins (ties broken by most VP). Second, if any player has 50 or more victory points, that player wins (ties broken by highest Threat). Third, if five regions have been ruined, the player with the most VP wins (ties broken by Threat). Fourth, if the Old World deck is empty, the Old World wins and all players lose. So there are two routes to victory: advancing your Threat dial to its Victory position, or accumulating victory points through domination and ruination.

walkthrough.title: Phase 1: The Old World Phase
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, draw one card from the Old World deck and carry out its italicized instructions immediately. The player with the lowest Threat makes any decisions required by the card, such as where to place tokens. After resolving the instructions, place the card face up in the leftmost space of the Old World track on the board. If there was already a card in space 1, slide it to space 2. If there was already a card in space 2, remove it from the game entirely. Some cards say to discard them instead of adding them to the track. Some Old World cards have persistent effects that trigger later in the round or remain active while on the track. Watch for the Twin-tailed Comet icon on certain cards, which can cause other cards with that icon to be removed from the track.

walkthrough.title: Phase 2: The Draw Phase
walkthrough.text: During the draw phase, each player draws Chaos cards from their own personal deck according to the instructions on their Power sheet. Each Power draws a different number of cards. If your deck runs out, shuffle your discards to form a fresh deck. There is no hand size limit, so you keep everything. Also during this phase, reset your power marker to the highlighted starting space on your Power sheet's power point track. If you have purchased upgrade cards that give extra power points, move your marker further. You never carry over unused power points from previous rounds. Because these actions are independent, all players can do this simultaneously.

walkthrough.title: Phase 3: The Summoning Phase — Overview
walkthrough.text: The summoning phase is where the action happens. Players take turns in power order: Khorne, then Nurgle, then Tzeentch, then Slaanesh, then back to Khorne, cycling until everyone is out of power points. On each turn, you may either summon one follower figure to the board, or play one Chaos card. Both cost power points, which you track by moving your power marker leftward. If you do not wish to act, move your power marker to 0 and your turn is skipped for the rest of the phase. When all players are at 0, the summoning phase ends.

walkthrough.title: Summoning Follower Figures
walkthrough.text: To summon a follower, choose one of your plastic figures, either from your supply or already on the board (effectively moving it). Pay its summoning cost by moving your power marker left. Then place the figure in any region where you already have a figure, or in a region adjacent to one where you have a figure. Adjacent regions are those sharing a border. If you have no figures on the board at all, you may place your first figure in any region without restriction. Each Power has three types of followers. Cultists are cheap but have 0 attack; they exist to place corruption tokens. Warriors are stronger fighters with higher attack values. Greater Daemons are the most powerful but expensive to summon.

walkthrough.title: Playing Chaos Cards
walkthrough.text: To play a Chaos card, choose a card from your hand, pay its cost in power points, and place it on an empty card space on any of the board's nine regions. Each region has two card spaces, one on each side of its name. If both spaces in a region are full, you cannot play a card there. If all 18 spaces on the board are full, you cannot play any Chaos card. When you play a card, immediately carry out its effects. The printed cost of a Chaos card also contributes to your domination value in that region during the corruption phase. Card effects are mandatory if possible. The effects of two cards with the same name in the same region are generally cumulative. Each Power's 24 Chaos cards are unique to that god and reflect their playstyle.

walkthrough.title: Phase 4: The Battle Phase
walkthrough.text: After summoning, battle is resolved region by region in standard region order: Norsca, Troll Country, Kislev, The Empire, Bretonnia, Estalia, Tilea, Border Princes, Badlands. Battle occurs in any region where at least one player can roll dice and has a legal target. In each region, players roll dice in power order. To calculate your dice, total the attack values of all your figures in that region. Roll that many battle dice. Each result of 4, 5, or 6 scores one hit. Every 6 also explodes, meaning you roll one additional die immediately, which can also hit and explode. There is no limit to explosions.

walkthrough.title: Assigning Hits in Battle
walkthrough.text: After rolling all your dice including explosions, assign each hit to an enemy figure or Peasant token in the same region. You cannot hit your own figures. If the total hits assigned to a figure equal or exceed its defense value, that figure is tipped on its side, meaning it is killed. However, tipped figures are not removed until all players have rolled for that region, so a tipped figure can still roll its own attack dice. Peasant tokens only take one hit to kill and are removed immediately, placed on the killing player's Power sheet. You cannot store up hits or gang up with another player on a tough figure. Excess hits with no legal target are simply lost. Some Chaos cards grant early hits at the beginning of battle, which can kill figures before they roll.

walkthrough.title: Phase 5: Corruption Phase — Domination Step
walkthrough.text: The corruption phase has two steps. First is the domination step. Each region is checked in standard region order. For each region, calculate every player's domination value there by adding the number of their figures in that region plus the printed costs of any Chaos cards they played there. The player with the highest domination value compares it to the region's Resistance. If it strictly exceeds the Resistance, that player scores victory points equal to the region's Conquest Value. If two or more players tie for the highest domination value, nobody dominates and nobody scores, even if they all exceeded the Resistance. Ruined regions are skipped entirely during domination.

walkthrough.title: Phase 5: Corruption Phase — Corruption Step
walkthrough.text: After domination, the corruption step begins. Each region is checked in standard order again. For each region, every player places one corruption token per Cultist they have in that region. Then count the total corruption tokens from all players in that region. Warpstone tokens also count toward this total. If the total reaches 12 or more, the region is ruined. Place the next sequential ruination card on that region. Each player who placed at least one corruption token in that region during this step immediately scores the ruiner bonus listed on the ruination card. If no ruination cards remain, the region is not ruined. No corruption tokens are placed in already-ruined regions.

walkthrough.title: Phase 6: End Phase — Cards, Heroes, and Old World
walkthrough.text: The end phase has six steps, always in order. Step 1: Remove all Chaos cards from the board and place them in each player's discard pile. Step 2: Resolve Hero tokens. In each region with a Hero token, the player with the greatest Threat who has at least one figure there must remove one of their figures. This is done once per Hero token. Step 3: Resolve Old World cards. Check the Old World track for any persistent effects that say they resolve when Old World cards are resolved, and carry them out in track order.

walkthrough.title: Phase 6: End Phase — Ruination Scoring
walkthrough.text: Step 4: Score ruined regions. For each region that was ruined this round (has a face-up ruination card), the player with the most corruption tokens there scores the first-place value from the ruination card's table for that region. The player with the second-most scores the second-place value. If players tie for first, add the first and second values together, divide by the number of tied players, and round down. No non-tied players score. If players tie for second, divide the second-place value among them, rounding down. After scoring, flip the ruination card face down and remove all corruption tokens from the region, returning them to players' stockpiles.

walkthrough.title: Phase 6: End Phase — Threat Dial Advancement
walkthrough.text: Step 5: Advance Threat dials. Throughout the round, players earn dial advancement counters whenever they fulfill their Power's unique dial advancement condition. Khorne earns a counter by killing 2 or more enemy figures in the same region. Nurgle earns a counter by having 2 or more of his own corruption tokens already present when placing new corruption in a region. Tzeentch earns a counter by placing corruption in a region with 2 or more Warpstone tokens and magic symbols combined. Slaanesh earns a counter by placing 2 or more corruption tokens in the same region. Now, each player with at least one counter advances their dial one tick clockwise and carries out the revealed instruction. Then the player with the most counters gets one additional tick. Ties for most mean nobody gets the extra tick. Finally, return all counters to the stockpile.

walkthrough.title: Phase 6: End Phase — Dial Instructions and Game End
walkthrough.text: When your dial advances, the revealed instruction might be: Score X VP, Draw X Chaos Cards, Place X Nobles, Place X Warpstone, Remove X Corruption, Remove X Old World Tokens, or Upgrade Card (choose one of your unused upgrade cards and put it into play). The most critical instruction is Victory, which means that Power wins the game. Step 6: Check for game end. The four conditions are checked in order: dial at Victory, 50 or more VP, five regions ruined, or empty Old World deck. The game ends immediately when one condition is met. If the Old World deck is empty, all players lose.

summary.title: Game Structure and Victory Conditions
bullets: Game plays over multiple rounds, each with 6 phases
Win by Threat dial reaching Victory (ties: most VP)
Win by reaching 50+ VP (ties: highest Threat)
Win by most VP when 5 regions ruined (ties: highest Threat)
All players lose if Old World deck is emptied

summary.title: Phase 1: The Old World Phase
bullets: Draw 1 Old World card, carry out its italicized instructions immediately
Player with lowest Threat makes decisions (e.g., token placement)
Place card in slot 1 of Old World track, bump existing cards along
Card pushed past slot 2 is removed from game

summary.title: Phase 2: The Draw Phase
bullets: Each player draws Chaos cards per their Power sheet instructions
Reset power marker to highlighted starting value (+ any upgrade bonuses)
No hand size limit; shuffle discards if deck is empty
All players act simultaneously

summary.title: Phase 3: The Summoning Phase — Overview
bullets: Players take turns in power order: Khorne, Nurgle, Tzeentch, Slaanesh, cycling
Each turn: summon 1 follower OR play 1 Chaos card
Spend power points by moving marker leftward
Phase ends when all players reach 0 power points

summary.title: Summoning Follower Figures
bullets: Choose a figure from supply or reposition one already on board
Pay summoning cost in power points
Place in a region where you have a figure, or adjacent to one
First figure of the round (if none on board) can go anywhere
Three types: Cultists (cheap, 0 attack), Warriors (combat), Greater Daemons (powerful, expensive)

summary.title: Playing Chaos Cards
bullets: Pay card's cost, place on empty card space (2 per region, 18 total)
Carry out card effects immediately; effects are mandatory if possible
Card's printed cost also adds to your domination value in that region
Same-name cards in the same region are generally cumulative

summary.title: Phase 4: The Battle Phase
bullets: Battle resolved region by region in standard region order (Norsca to Badlands)
Each player totals attack values of their figures in the region = battle dice
Roll dice: 4/5/6 = hit; 6 also explodes (roll 1 more die, unlimited chain)
Players roll in power order within each region

summary.title: Assigning Hits in Battle
bullets: Assign each hit to an enemy figure or Peasant token in the same region
Hits >= defense value: figure is tipped (killed), removed after all players roll
Peasants die in 1 hit and go to the killer's Power sheet immediately
Cannot gang up or store hits across players; excess hits are lost

summary.title: Phase 5: Corruption Phase — Domination Step
bullets: Each region checked in order; domination value = figures + Chaos card costs
Highest domination value exceeding region's Resistance scores its Conquest Value as VP
Ties for highest: nobody dominates, nobody scores
Ruined regions are skipped

summary.title: Phase 5: Corruption Phase — Corruption Step
bullets: Each player places 1 corruption token per Cultist in each region
If total corruption (all players + Warpstone) reaches 12+, region is ruined
Place next ruination card; ruiners (who placed this turn) score bonus VP
No corruption placed in already-ruined regions

summary.title: Phase 6: End Phase — Cards, Heroes, and Old World
bullets: Step 1: Remove all Chaos cards from board to discard piles
Step 2: Hero tokens — highest-Threat player with figures in that region loses one figure per Hero
Step 3: Resolve persistent effects on Old World track cards

summary.title: Phase 6: End Phase — Ruination Scoring
bullets: Step 4: Score each newly ruined region (face-up ruination card)
1st place (most corruption tokens) scores first value; 2nd place scores second value
Ties: sum tied places' values, divide by number tied, round down
Flip ruination card face-down, clear all corruption tokens from region

summary.title: Phase 6: End Phase — Threat Dial Advancement
bullets: Khorne: kill 2+ enemies in same region; Nurgle: 2+ own corruption already in region when placing more
Tzeentch: corrupt region with 2+ Warpstone/magic symbols; Slaanesh: place 2+ corruption in same region
Each player with counters: advance dial 1 tick, resolve instruction
Player with most counters (no tie): 1 bonus tick; then clear all counters

summary.title: Phase 6: End Phase — Dial Instructions and Game End
bullets: Dial instructions: Score VP, Draw Cards, Place Nobles/Warpstone, Remove Corruption/Tokens, Upgrade Card, or Victory
Upgrade Card: choose 1 unused upgrade, put into play (follower or Power upgrade)
Check game end in order: dial Victory, 50+ VP, 5 regions ruined, empty Old World deck
Empty deck = all players lose

walkthrough.title: Welcome and Meet the Chaos Gods
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with four players, one per Chaos Power. Player 1 is Khorne the Blood God, Player 2 is Nurgle the Plaguelord, Player 3 is Tzeentch the Changer of Ways, and Player 4 is Slaanesh the Prince of Pleasure. Follow along even if you have fewer players. We will cover every phase and highlight common mistakes. Ready to corrupt the Old World? Let us begin.

walkthrough.title: The Old World Phase Begins
walkthrough.text: The first round starts with the Old World phase. Draw the top card from the Old World deck. Let us say it places two Peasant tokens on the board and has a persistent effect. Since all players start at the same Threat level, choose any player to make the placement decisions. Place the tokens as instructed, then put the Old World card face up in slot 1 of the Old World track. Remember, these Peasant tokens are targets Khorne can kill in battle for dial advancement purposes.

walkthrough.title: The Draw Phase — Powering Up
walkthrough.text: Now everyone draws Chaos cards according to their Power sheet. Khorne draws 2 cards (bringing his hand to 5). Nurgle draws 3 cards (hand of 6). Tzeentch draws 2 cards (hand of 5). Slaanesh draws 2 cards (hand of 5). Each player also resets their power marker to their starting power point value. Everyone does this simultaneously, so no waiting around.

walkthrough.title: Summoning Phase — Khorne Deploys First
walkthrough.text: The summoning phase begins with Khorne. Player 1 looks at the board and spots a region with a Peasant token and some opponents likely to deploy nearby. Khorne spends 1 power point to summon a Bloodsworn cultist to that region. Why a cultist? Because Khorne needs at least one cultist somewhere to place corruption tokens during the corruption phase. Even the Blood God needs corruption to threaten ruination and score VP.

walkthrough.title: Nurgle Spreads the Plague
walkthrough.text: Player 2 plays Nurgle. Nurgle's strength is cheap, plentiful figures. He summons a Leper cultist to the Empire, which is a Populous region and has a high Conquest Value. Populous regions are referenced by many game effects and tend to be hotly contested. Nurgle plans to flood this region with cultists to dominate it and pile up corruption tokens.

walkthrough.title: Tzeentch Plays a Chaos Card
walkthrough.text: Player 3 plays Tzeentch. Instead of summoning a figure, Tzeentch plays a low-cost Chaos card to a region containing a Warpstone token. This is smart because Tzeentch's dial advancement condition requires corrupting regions with Warpstone tokens or magic symbols. Many Tzeentch Chaos cards also have magic symbols printed on them, which count toward this condition. By playing a card with a magic symbol to a Warpstone region, Tzeentch is already setting up their dial advancement for later.

walkthrough.title: Slaanesh Targets the Nobles
walkthrough.text: Player 4 plays Slaanesh. Slaanesh's dial advancement condition triggers when placing 2 or more corruption tokens in the same region, but Slaanesh also specifically targets regions with Noble and Hero tokens. Player 4 summons two Seductress cultists to a region with a Noble token, spending 2 power points total. Two cultists means two corruption tokens during the corruption step, which will trigger Slaanesh's dial advancement condition.

walkthrough.title: Khorne's Second Turn — The Mistake
walkthrough.text: Back to Khorne. Player 1 is eager for battle and summons a Bloodletter warrior to a distant region with no enemies. The Bloodletter costs 2 power points and has an attack value of 2, making it a strong fighter. But here is the problem: there is nobody to fight there. Khorne needs to kill 2 or more enemies in the same region to earn dial advancement counters. A lone Bloodletter in an empty region generates no kills, no dial ticks, and no domination either because Khorne only has the one figure there.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Khorne Must Hunt, Not Hide
walkthrough.text: The takeaway for Khorne: your figures need enemies to kill. Do not place warriors in empty regions hoping opponents will come to you. Instead, deploy where opponents already have figures, or where they are likely to summon cultists. Khorne earns dial advancement by killing 2 or more enemies in the same region, so you need to be where the targets are. Spread your forces across regions where opponents have vulnerable cultists.

walkthrough.title: Continuing the Summoning Phase
walkthrough.text: The summoning phase continues cycling through the players. Nurgle summons more Lepers to Populous regions, building domination presence. Tzeentch plays another free Chaos card with a magic symbol, stacking up symbols in a key region. Slaanesh plays a Dark Influence card to move a Noble token closer to her cultists. Each player keeps summoning figures and playing cards until their power points are at 0. Watch how Tzeentch conserves power by playing free and low-cost cards early, letting opponents commit first.

walkthrough.title: Slaanesh's Mistake — Ignoring Defense
walkthrough.text: Player 4 placed all of Slaanesh's cultists in a single region with a Noble token, hoping for maximum corruption. But Khorne noticed and sent a Bloodletter there. Slaanesh's Seductress cultists have a defense value of only 1, meaning a single hit kills each one. Khorne's Bloodletter rolls 2 attack dice and is very likely to kill both Seductresses. If Slaanesh loses both cultists, no corruption tokens are placed and no dial advancement happens.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Spread Your Cultists for Safety
walkthrough.text: The lesson here: do not stack all your cultists in one region where Khorne can slaughter them. Spread cultists across two or three regions so you are not wiped out in a single battle. Slaanesh in particular needs to be flexible and opportunistic. If Khorne is threatening a region, consider deploying elsewhere or using Chaos cards to protect your followers. Losing all your cultists in one battle is devastating.

walkthrough.title: The Battle Phase — Blood Flows
walkthrough.text: The battle phase begins. Battles are resolved region by region in standard region order. In the region where Khorne's Bloodletter faces Slaanesh's two Seductresses, Khorne rolls first because Khorne always acts first in power order. With 2 attack dice, Player 1 rolls a 4 and a 6. That is 2 hits, and the 6 explodes for another die. The explosion roll is a 3, so no additional hit. Two hits is enough to kill both Seductresses since each has a defense of 1. Slaanesh's cultists are tipped and will be removed after all players roll in this region.

walkthrough.title: Slaanesh's Mistake — Forgetting That Battle Kills Cultists
walkthrough.text: Player 4 is frustrated. Both Seductresses are dead, which means no corruption tokens will be placed in that region during the corruption phase and no dial advancement counter earned. Player 4 forgot a critical rule: you need your cultists to survive the battle phase in order to place corruption during the corruption phase. Cultists have 0 attack value, so they cannot fight back. They need warriors or Chaos cards to protect them.

walkthrough.title: Corruption Phase — Domination
walkthrough.text: Now we enter the corruption phase. First, the domination step. In each region, calculate every player's domination value: number of your figures plus the printed costs of your Chaos cards there. Compare the highest value to the region's Resistance. Nurgle has three Lepers and a Chaos card in the Empire, giving a domination value of 3 figures plus the card's cost. If Nurgle's value exceeds the Empire's Resistance, Nurgle scores the Empire's Conquest Value as victory points. Populous regions like the Empire tend to have high Conquest Values, making them worth fighting for.

walkthrough.title: Corruption Phase — Placing Corruption
walkthrough.text: Next, the corruption step. Each player places one corruption token per cultist in each region. Nurgle's three Lepers in the Empire place three corruption tokens. Khorne's lone Bloodsworn places one. Tzeentch's Acolyte places one. After placing, count the total corruption in each region. This is the early game so no regions are near 12 yet, but watch these numbers climb each round. Each player who fulfills their dial advancement condition during this step places a dial advancement counter on their Threat dial.

walkthrough.title: End Phase — Cleaning Up and Advancing
walkthrough.text: Now the end phase. Step 1: Remove all Chaos cards from the board to discard piles. Step 2: Check for Hero tokens and remove figures if applicable. Step 3: Resolve any Old World card persistent effects. Step 4: Score any newly ruined regions. None are ruined this early. Step 5: Advance Threat dials. Khorne killed 2 enemies in one region, earning one dial advancement counter. Nurgle placed corruption where he already had tokens, earning a counter. Each advances their dial one tick and carries out the instruction. Khorne had the most counters, so if there is no tie, Khorne gets a bonus tick.

walkthrough.title: Understanding the Dial Instructions
walkthrough.text: When Khorne's dial advances, the revealed instruction might say Score 4 VP or Upgrade Card. Upgrade cards are game-changers. Khorne might upgrade his Bloodletters to roll even more dice in combat, or upgrade his Bloodsworn cultists to be tougher. When the instruction says Upgrade Card, choose one of your five upgrade cards and place it into play. Follower upgrades slide over the matching stats on your Power sheet, permanently improving those figures for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Check for Game End
walkthrough.text: Step 6: Check for game end. After round 1, it is extremely unlikely that any end condition is met. No dial is at Victory, nobody has 50 VP, no five regions are ruined, and the Old World deck still has cards. The round is over. A new round begins with another Old World phase. The game typically lasts 6 to 8 rounds, getting more intense as corruption piles up and dials advance.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have now played through a complete first round of Chaos in the Old World. You have seen all six phases in action, watched Khorne waste a warrior in an empty region, and seen Slaanesh lose all her cultists to a preventable battle. Remember: Khorne needs to hunt where the enemies are, spread your cultists for safety, and always plan your summoning with the battle phase in mind. Each Chaos god plays very differently, and that asymmetry is what makes this game special.

walkthrough.title: Before You Unleash Chaos
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics for each Chaos god and common mistakes to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every game term, token type, region, and Chaos god ability.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. May the Dark Gods favor you!

summary.title: Welcome and Meet the Chaos Gods
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 4 players: Khorne, Nurgle, Tzeentch, Slaanesh
Covers a complete first round through all 6 phases
Follow along even with fewer players

summary.title: The Old World Phase Begins
bullets: Draw 1 Old World card, resolve its immediate instructions
Lowest-Threat player makes placement decisions
Place card on Old World track slot 1

summary.title: The Draw Phase — Powering Up
bullets: Each player draws Chaos cards per their Power sheet
Reset power marker to starting value
All players act simultaneously

summary.title: Summoning Phase — Khorne Deploys First
bullets: Khorne summons a Bloodsworn cultist to a region with targets
Even Khorne needs cultists for corruption placement
Spend 1 power point, place adjacent to existing figure or anywhere if first figure

summary.title: Nurgle Spreads the Plague
bullets: Nurgle summons cheap Leper cultists to Populous regions
Populous regions have high Conquest Values for domination scoring
Plan to flood regions with cultists for domination + corruption

summary.title: Tzeentch Plays a Chaos Card
bullets: Tzeentch plays a free Chaos card with a magic symbol to a Warpstone region
Sets up dial advancement condition (Warpstone + magic symbols)
Low-cost cards let Tzeentch conserve power and act last

summary.title: Slaanesh Targets the Nobles
bullets: Slaanesh summons 2 Seductress cultists to a Noble token region
2 cultists = 2 corruption tokens = triggers dial advancement condition
Slaanesh needs Noble and Hero tokens for optimal play

summary.title: Khorne's Second Turn — The Mistake
bullets: Khorne summons a Bloodletter to an empty region with no enemies
No enemies = no kills = no dial advancement = wasted power points
A strong warrior with nothing to fight is useless

summary.title: Lesson: Khorne Must Hunt, Not Hide
bullets: Deploy where opponents have vulnerable cultists
Khorne needs 2+ kills per region for dial advancement
Spread forces across occupied regions, not empty ones

summary.title: Continuing the Summoning Phase
bullets: Players continue cycling until all power points are spent
Nurgle floods Populous regions; Tzeentch plays free cards
Slaanesh uses Dark Influence to move Noble tokens closer

summary.title: Slaanesh's Mistake — Ignoring Defense
bullets: Slaanesh stacked all cultists in one region where Khorne deployed a warrior
Seductress cultists have defense 1 — each dies to a single hit
Khorne's Bloodletter (2 attack dice) will likely kill both

summary.title: Lesson: Spread Your Cultists for Safety
bullets: Spread cultists across 2-3 regions to survive Khorne's attacks
Dead cultists = no corruption tokens = no dial advancement
Use Chaos cards to protect key cultists or avoid contested regions

summary.title: The Battle Phase — Blood Flows
bullets: Khorne rolls first (power order) with 2 dice for his Bloodletter
Rolls 4 and 6 = 2 hits + 1 explosion die; kills both Seductresses
Tipped figures removed after all players roll for the region

summary.title: Slaanesh's Mistake — Forgetting That Battle Kills Cultists
bullets: Dead cultists place no corruption tokens in the corruption phase
Cultists have 0 attack — they cannot fight back
Always plan summoning with the upcoming battle phase in mind

summary.title: Corruption Phase — Domination
bullets: Calculate domination value: your figures + Chaos card costs in each region
Highest value exceeding Resistance scores the region's Conquest Value as VP
Ties for highest = nobody scores

summary.title: Corruption Phase — Placing Corruption
bullets: Place 1 corruption token per cultist in each region
Total corruption (all players + Warpstone) reaching 12 = ruination
Dial advancement counters placed when conditions are met

summary.title: End Phase — Cleaning Up and Advancing
bullets: Remove Chaos cards, resolve Heroes and Old World effects
Score any newly ruined regions, advance Threat dials
Player with most dial counters (no tie) gets a bonus tick

summary.title: Understanding the Dial Instructions
bullets: Dial ticks reveal instructions: Score VP, Upgrade Card, Place tokens, etc.
Upgrade cards permanently improve your figures or Power abilities
Follower upgrades replace stats on your Power sheet

summary.title: Check for Game End
bullets: After round 1, no end conditions are met — game continues
Check in order: dial Victory, 50 VP, 5 ruined regions, empty deck
Games typically last 6-8 rounds

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You have played through all 6 phases of a complete round
Saw Khorne waste a warrior and Slaanesh lose all cultists
Key lessons: hunt where enemies are, spread cultists, plan for battle

summary.title: Before You Unleash Chaos
bullets: Check out General Tips for god-specific strategy basics
Reference the Appendix for every term, token, and ability
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime via the Q&A tab

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the biggest beginner traps. First, ignoring dial advancement. Many new players focus only on victory points and forget that the Threat dial is often the fastest route to winning. Second, stacking all your cultists in one region where they get slaughtered in battle. Spread them out. Third, not checking the Old World card each round. Those cards spawn tokens, move heroes, and create effects that can ruin your plans or create opportunities. Fourth, forgetting that domination ties mean nobody scores. If two players have the same domination value, neither gets the VP, even if both exceeded the Resistance.

walkthrough.title: Know Your Win Condition
walkthrough.text: Each Chaos god has a natural path to victory. Khorne wins most often by Threat dial advancement. His dial condition requires killing 2 or more enemies in the same region, and his warriors are the best fighters in the game. Nurgle wins most often by victory points, dominating Populous regions and scoring big when they are ruined. Tzeentch can win by either route, with strong Chaos cards and a flexible dial condition involving Warpstone and magic symbols. Slaanesh wins by opportunism, pivoting between dial advancement and VP based on how the game unfolds. Know your god's natural strength and lean into it.

walkthrough.title: Khorne Beginner Tips
walkthrough.text: Khorne is all about battle. Your Bloodletter warriors roll 2 dice each, which is unique among all powers. Deploy them where enemies have vulnerable cultists. You need to kill 2 or more enemies in the same region to earn dial advancement counters, so target regions where multiple opponents have figures. Do not waste power points summoning to empty regions. Your cultists are few (only 4 Bloodsworn), so use them sparingly for corruption in regions you control through force. Try to earn 2 dial ticks per round.

walkthrough.title: Nurgle Beginner Tips
walkthrough.text: Nurgle's strength is cheap, plentiful figures. With 6 Leper cultists and 5 Plaguebearers, you have more bodies than anyone else. Focus on Populous regions where the Conquest Values are highest. Your dial advancement condition requires having 2 or more of your own corruption tokens already present when you place new corruption. This means you need to build up over multiple rounds in the same regions. Do not spread too thin. Domination is your bread and butter, so fight for regions with high Conquest Values. Your Chaos cards help prevent ruination of regions you control, giving you more rounds to dominate.

walkthrough.title: Tzeentch Beginner Tips
walkthrough.text: Tzeentch has the best Chaos deck in the game, with many free and low-cost cards. You draw enough cards each round to replace what you play, so play as many as you can every round. Pay attention to regions with Warpstone tokens. They are key to your dial advancement condition, which requires corrupting regions with 2 or more Warpstone tokens and magic symbols combined. Many of your Chaos cards have magic symbols printed on them. Conserve power points early in each summoning phase by playing free cards first and letting opponents commit their resources.

walkthrough.title: Slaanesh Beginner Tips
walkthrough.text: Slaanesh is the most flexible power. Your dial advancement condition triggers when you place 2 or more corruption tokens in the same region, which is straightforward with multiple cultists. Focus on regions with Noble and Hero tokens early, as they fuel your strategy. Use your Dark Influence cards to move Noble tokens to regions where you are strong. Your Seductresses are fragile, so avoid Khorne's warriors. If Noble and Hero tokens appear early through Old World cards, push for a dial win. If Khorne is strong or opponents leave valuable regions uncontested, pivot to a VP win.

walkthrough.title: Managing the Summoning Phase
walkthrough.text: The summoning phase is the most strategic part of each round. Every decision you make is visible to all other players, and the cycling turn order means you need to react to what others do. Key principles: summon cultists where you want corruption, summon warriors where you want to kill. Play Chaos cards early if they are free, because they cost nothing and establish your presence. Save expensive summons for later in the cycle when you know where opponents have committed. Remember that repositioning a figure already on the board costs the same as summoning a new one.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Ruination Timing
walkthrough.text: Regions are ruined when their total corruption (all players plus Warpstone tokens) reaches 12 or more. Ruination is a massive scoring event. The players who placed corruption this turn score the ruiner bonus from the ruination card, and then in the end phase, first and second place in corruption score additional VP based on the region. Early ruinations are worth fewer points; later ruinations are worth more. Once a region is ruined, no more corruption can be placed there and domination no longer scores. Time your corruption so you are in position to score big when ruination happens.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Do not ignore Threat dial advancement — it is often the fastest win
Do not stack all cultists in one region where they get wiped out
Read the Old World card every round — tokens and effects change the board
Domination ties = nobody scores; avoid wasting resources in tied regions

summary.title: Know Your Win Condition
bullets: Khorne: usually wins by Threat dial (killing enemies)
Nurgle: usually wins by VP (domination + ruination scoring)
Tzeentch: flexible, can win by either route (Warpstone/magic dial condition)
Slaanesh: opportunistic, pivots based on game state (Nobles/Heroes)

summary.title: Khorne Beginner Tips
bullets: Bloodletters roll 2 dice each — best fighters in the game
Deploy where enemies cluster; need 2+ kills per region for dial ticks
Do not summon to empty regions — warriors need targets
Use your few cultists (4 Bloodsworn) carefully for key corruption

summary.title: Nurgle Beginner Tips
bullets: Most figures of any power (12 total); flood Populous regions
Dial condition needs 2+ own corruption already present when placing more
Focus domination on high Conquest Value regions; avoid overextending
Chaos cards can prevent premature ruination of your best regions

summary.title: Tzeentch Beginner Tips
bullets: Best Chaos deck — many free/low-cost cards with magic symbols
Draw enough to replace what you play; play as many as possible each round
Target Warpstone regions for dial advancement
Conserve power early; let opponents commit first

summary.title: Slaanesh Beginner Tips
bullets: Most flexible power; dial triggers from 2+ corruption in same region
Focus on Noble/Hero token regions early; use Dark Influence to reposition Nobles
Avoid Khorne's warriors — Seductresses are fragile (defense 1)
Pivot between dial win and VP win based on game state

summary.title: Managing the Summoning Phase
bullets: Summon cultists where you want corruption; warriors where you want kills
Play free Chaos cards early to establish presence without spending power
Save expensive summons until opponents commit their resources
Repositioning an existing figure costs the same as summoning a new one

summary.title: Understanding Ruination Timing
bullets: Region ruined at 12+ total corruption (all players + Warpstone)
Ruiners (placed corruption this turn) score bonus VP
1st/2nd place in corruption score additional VP from ruination card
Later ruinations are worth more points; ruined regions are dead for domination

walkthrough.title: Khorne: Dial Rush Strategy
walkthrough.text: Khorne's fastest path to victory is dial advancement. Your condition is killing 2 or more enemy figures in the same region. The key insight is that you need to spread your warriors across multiple regions where opponents have clustered figures, rather than concentrating in one massive battle. If you kill 2 enemies in three different regions, that is three dial advancement counters in a single round. With the bonus tick for having the most counters, that is four total ticks. At that pace, you can reach Victory in three rounds. Prioritize Nurgle's cheap Lepers and Slaanesh's fragile Seductresses as targets. Avoid chasing Tzeentch's figures unless they are isolated, because Tzeentch has cards that teleport figures away.

walkthrough.title: Khorne: Upgrade Timing
walkthrough.text: Khorne's upgrades are devastating. Upgrading Bloodletters gives them early hits (rolling dice before other figures), which can kill enemies before they fight back. Upgrading Bloodsworn increases their combat capability. Khorne's Power upgrades give extra power points or cards. The key decision is which upgrade to take first. If opponents have many weak figures, upgrade Bloodletters for early hits. If you need more summoning capacity, take a Power upgrade for extra power points. Time your upgrades to maximize their impact over remaining rounds.

walkthrough.title: Nurgle: Domination Engine
walkthrough.text: Nurgle's optimal strategy is building an unstoppable domination engine in two or three Populous regions. With 6 Lepers and 5 Plaguebearers, Nurgle can establish overwhelming domination values that other players cannot easily contest. The trick is that your dial condition requires having 2 or more of your own corruption tokens already present when you place more. This means you score dial ticks in rounds 2 and beyond in regions where you have been corrupting. Front-load your corruption in Populous regions early. Even modest dial advancement is valuable for the upgrade cards and VP it provides.

walkthrough.title: Nurgle: Ruination Control
walkthrough.text: Nurgle's Chaos cards include abilities that remove corruption tokens or prevent ruination. This is enormously powerful because you want to dominate regions for VP over multiple rounds before they are ruined. If you can delay ruination of a region where you have 8+ corruption tokens and consistent domination, you score VP from domination each round AND score massive first-place VP when it finally is ruined. Control the pace of corruption: place enough to advance your dial, but use your cards to prevent premature ruination.

walkthrough.title: Tzeentch: Card Advantage Strategy
walkthrough.text: Tzeentch's draw ability is unmatched. You can cycle through your deck faster than any other Power, seeing more of your cards and having more options each round. Your optimal play pattern is: play free and low-cost Chaos cards first to burn through your deck, save power points for late summons when you see what opponents do, and target Warpstone regions for dial advancement. Many of your cards have magic symbols, and your dial condition counts both Warpstone tokens and magic symbols in a region. A region with 1 Warpstone and 1 of your magic-symbol cards already meets the threshold.

walkthrough.title: Tzeentch: Dual Path to Victory
walkthrough.text: Tzeentch is unique in being equally capable of winning by dial advancement or VP. Keep both paths open as long as possible. If Warpstone tokens are concentrated in a few regions, push for dial. If regions are ruining quickly and you have corruption in the right places, push for VP. Your Teleport card lets you reposition figures to exploit openings that appear after other players commit. Your Warp Shield card protects key figures from Khorne's attacks. Flexibility is your greatest weapon; do not commit to one path until mid-game.

walkthrough.title: Slaanesh: Noble Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Slaanesh's Dark Influence cards can move Noble tokens from one region to another. This is a game-changing ability because you can pull Nobles into regions where you already have cultists, setting up dial advancement without having to go to the Nobles. In the early game, identify where the 2 starting Nobles are and either deploy there or move them to you. Noble tokens also increase a region's Conquest Value by 1, so moving Nobles to regions you dominate earns you extra VP. Combine this with your Perverse Infiltration cards for surprise ruination scoring.

walkthrough.title: Slaanesh: Opponent Exploitation
walkthrough.text: Slaanesh has unique cards that let you control enemy figures temporarily. When you control an enemy cultist, their corruption tokens placed are in your color, effectively stealing corruption from opponents. This is devastating near ruination thresholds. Use control effects to swing closely contested regions in your favor, both for domination and for corruption placement position. Slaanesh also benefits from acting last in power order, because you see where everyone else committed before you make your final decisions.

walkthrough.title: Metagame: Reading the Board State
walkthrough.text: Advanced play requires reading the board constantly. Track every player's dial position. Know who is closest to Victory and who needs to be stopped. Count corruption tokens in each region and anticipate which regions will ruin next round. Watch the Old World deck: how many cards remain? If only two or three cards are left, the game will end soon by deck exhaustion unless someone wins first. When the deck is running low, aggression increases because nobody wants the mutual loss. Monitor other players' upgrade status: an upgraded Bloodletter is far more dangerous than a basic one.

walkthrough.title: Metagame: Threat Level Politics
walkthrough.text: Threat determines two critical things: who makes decisions on Old World card effects (lowest Threat) and who loses figures to Hero tokens (highest Threat with figures present). Managing your Threat level is a subtle but powerful strategic tool. Being at low Threat lets you decide where new Old World tokens are placed, giving you information and control. Being at high Threat makes you vulnerable to Hero tokens but also means your dial is more advanced. In the mid-game, the player with the highest Threat is often the biggest target for the table. Use this dynamic to deflect attention when you are ahead and to rally opposition against a leader.

summary.title: Khorne: Dial Rush Strategy
bullets: Spread warriors across multiple regions with enemy figures
3 regions with 2+ kills = 3 counters + bonus tick = 4 ticks in one round
Target Nurgle's Lepers and Slaanesh's Seductresses (fragile, plentiful)
Avoid chasing Tzeentch — Teleport cards let him escape

summary.title: Khorne: Upgrade Timing
bullets: Bloodletter upgrade: early hits (roll before others), devastating vs. cultists
Power upgrade: extra power points for more summons per round
Choose first upgrade based on opponent composition
Time upgrades to maximize remaining rounds of impact

summary.title: Nurgle: Domination Engine
bullets: Build domination in 2-3 Populous regions with high Conquest Values
Dial condition needs 2+ own corruption already present — invest early
12 figures total lets you overwhelm contested regions
Even modest dial advancement yields upgrades and VP bonuses

summary.title: Nurgle: Ruination Control
bullets: Chaos cards can remove corruption or block ruination
Delay ruination to score domination VP over multiple rounds
Score massive 1st-place ruination VP when region finally falls
Control corruption pace: enough for dial, not enough for premature ruin

summary.title: Tzeentch: Card Advantage Strategy
bullets: Cycle deck faster than any other Power with superior draw ability
Play free/cheap cards first; save power for late-round summons
Magic symbols on cards + Warpstone tokens = dial advancement
1 Warpstone + 1 magic symbol card = threshold met

summary.title: Tzeentch: Dual Path to Victory
bullets: Equally viable for dial or VP win — keep both paths open
Teleport repositions figures after opponents commit
Warp Shield protects key figures from Khorne's attacks
Commit to one path mid-game based on board state

summary.title: Slaanesh: Noble Manipulation
bullets: Dark Influence cards move Noble tokens to your regions
Nobles increase Conquest Value by 1 — extra VP for domination
Perverse Infiltration cards score surprise ruination points
Control the Noble economy to fuel dial advancement

summary.title: Slaanesh: Opponent Exploitation
bullets: Control enemy cultists to place corruption in YOUR color
Swing closely contested regions for domination and ruination
Last in power order = see all opponents' commitments first
Devastating near ruination thresholds — steal 1st place corruption

summary.title: Metagame: Reading the Board State
bullets: Track every player's dial position — who is closest to Victory?
Count corruption per region — anticipate next ruination
Monitor Old World deck size — low deck = urgency to win before all lose
Watch upgrade status — upgraded Bloodletters are far deadlier

summary.title: Metagame: Threat Level Politics
bullets: Lowest Threat = decides Old World card placement (information + control)
Highest Threat = loses figures to Hero tokens (vulnerability)
High-Threat player is often the biggest table target mid-game
Use Threat dynamics to deflect attention or rally opposition

entries.definition: The Blood God, the Skulltaker. One of the four Ruinous Powers. Khorne's forces are the strongest in combat, with Bloodletters rolling 2 attack dice each. Khorne's dial advancement condition is killing 2 or more enemy figures in the same region. Khorne's color is red.

The Plaguelord, the Father of Corruption. One of the four Ruinous Powers. Nurgle has the most figures (12 total) and excels at domination through cheap, plentiful followers. Nurgle's dial advancement condition is having 2 or more of his own corruption tokens already in a region when placing additional corruption. Nurgle's color is green.

The Changer of Ways, the Great Conspirator. One of the four Ruinous Powers. Tzeentch has the best Chaos card deck with many free and low-cost cards, plus strong draw ability. Tzeentch's dial advancement condition is placing corruption in a region with 2 or more combined Warpstone tokens and magic symbols. Tzeentch's color is blue.

The Prince of Pleasure and Pain, the Lord of Temptations. One of the four Ruinous Powers. Slaanesh is the most flexible power, with cards that manipulate Noble tokens and control enemy figures. Slaanesh's dial advancement condition is placing 2 or more corruption tokens in the same region. Slaanesh's color is purple.

The collective name for the four Chaos gods: Khorne, Nurgle, Tzeentch, and Slaanesh. Also called Chaos Powers, Powers, or gods. Each has unique figures, Chaos cards, upgrade cards, and a distinct path to victory.
Khorne's Greater Daemon. The most powerful individual figure in Khorne's army, with high attack and defense values but expensive to summon. Only one is available per game.

Khorne's Warriors. Each has an attack value of 2, making them the best fighters among all Warrior types. Khorne has 6 Bloodletters. They can be upgraded to gain early hits (rolling before other figures in battle).
Khorne's Cultists. Used for placing corruption tokens. Khorne has only 4 Bloodsworn, the fewest cultists of any Power. They cost 1 power point to summon and have 0 attack value.
Nurgle's Greater Daemon. A powerful but expensive figure with high defense (3), making it very hard to kill. Only one is available. Its high attack value makes it a strong combat presence.
Nurgle's Warriors. Sturdy fighters that support Nurgle's domination strategy. Nurgle has 5 Plaguebearers. They have moderate attack and defense values.
Nurgle's Cultists. Cheap to summon with 0 attack value. Nurgle has 6 Lepers, the most cultists of any Power, making Nurgle the best at placing corruption tokens en masse.
Tzeentch's Greater Daemon. A powerful figure that is expensive to summon. Only one is available. Used as a combat deterrent and domination tool.
Tzeentch's Warriors. Tzeentch has only 3 Horrors, the fewest warriors of any Power. They provide combat support and domination value.
Tzeentch's Cultists. Tzeentch has 8 Acolytes, the most cultists of any Power. They cost 1 power point each to summon and have 0 attack. Used to place corruption in Warpstone/magic-symbol regions.
Slaanesh's Greater Daemon. A powerful figure with high attack and defense. Only one is available. Expensive to summon but provides strong combat presence and domination value.
Slaanesh's Warriors. Slaanesh has only 3 Daemonettes. When upgraded, they can only be damaged by battle die results of 6, making them extremely hard to kill.
Slaanesh's Cultists. Slaanesh has 6 Seductresses. They have low defense (1 hit kills them) and 0 attack. They are used to place corruption tokens in regions with Noble and Hero tokens.

The cheapest follower class. All Cultists have 0 attack value and cannot fight. Their purpose is to place corruption tokens during the corruption phase (1 token per Cultist per region). Each Power's Cultists have a unique name.

A mid-tier follower class with moderate attack and defense values. Warriors provide combat capability and contribute to domination value. Each Power's Warriors have a unique name and slightly different statistics.
The most powerful follower class. Each Power has exactly one Greater Daemon with high attack, high defense, and high summoning cost. Greater Daemons are major combat threats and domination anchors.

The standard order in which players act when actions are interdependent: Khorne first, Nurgle second, Tzeentch third, Slaanesh fourth. This applies to summoning, battle resolution, corruption placement, and more.

The currency used to summon followers and play Chaos cards during the summoning phase. Each player's power points reset to their starting value (shown on the Power sheet) at the beginning of each round's draw phase. Upgrade cards can increase the starting value.
A token placed on the power point track of each player's Power sheet to track remaining power points during the summoning phase. Moved leftward as power is spent.

A dial attached to the game board for each Power, tracking that Power's progress toward victory through dial advancement. The main window reveals progressive benefits (VP, upgrades, etc.), and the smaller window shows the Power's current Threat value. Advancing to the Victory instruction wins the game.

A numerical value shown in the smaller window of each Power's Threat dial. Threat determines who makes decisions on Old World card effects (lowest Threat decides) and who is targeted by Hero tokens (highest Threat loses figures). Threat also breaks ties for the 50 VP and 5-ruined-regions victory conditions.
A single-unit clockwise advancement of a Threat dial. Each tick reveals a new instruction in the main window and updates the Threat value in the smaller window.

A token placed on a Power's Threat dial whenever that Power fulfills its unique dial advancement condition during a round. At the end of the round, counters are cashed in for dial ticks. The player with the most counters earns one bonus tick. A single event cannot generate multiple counters.

Each Power has a unique condition printed on their Power sheet. Khorne: kill 2+ enemy figures in the same region. Nurgle: have 2+ own corruption already present when placing more. Tzeentch: corrupt a region with 2+ combined Warpstone tokens and magic symbols. Slaanesh: place 2+ corruption tokens in the same region.

During the domination step of the corruption phase, the player with the highest domination value in a region that strictly exceeds the region's Resistance scores VP equal to the Conquest Value. Domination value equals the number of your figures plus the printed costs of your Chaos cards in that region. Ties mean nobody dominates.

A calculated value for each player in each region: the number of that player's figures plus the sum of printed costs on their Chaos cards in the region. Used to determine who dominates a region during the corruption phase.

A region's defensive statistic, printed as the number on the region. A player's domination value must strictly exceed the Resistance to dominate. Resistance can be modified by Skaven tokens (decrease by 1 each) but these modifications do not affect Conquest Value.

The number of victory points scored by the player who dominates a region. Printed as the same number as Resistance on the board, but can be independently modified. Noble tokens increase Conquest Value by 1 each, without affecting Resistance.

Tokens in each Power's color placed in regions during the corruption step (1 per Cultist). When a region accumulates 12 or more total corruption tokens (including Warpstone tokens), the region is ruined. Each Power has 29 corruption tokens; the supply is considered unlimited.

When a region's total corruption tokens (all players plus Warpstone) reach 12 or more, the region is ruined. A ruination card is placed on it, ruiners score bonus VP, and later in the end phase, 1st and 2nd place in corruption score additional VP. Once ruined, no new Chaos cards, Old World tokens, domination scoring, or corruption placement occur in that region.

One of 5 cards placed on regions when they are ruined, in sequential order (1 through 5). Each card lists the ruiner bonus and a table of VP values for 1st and 2nd place corruption by region. Later ruination cards generally award more VP. When scored, the card is flipped face-down and all corruption tokens are cleared.

Standard six-sided dice used during the battle phase. Results of 4, 5, or 6 score hits. A result of 6 also explodes, granting one additional die roll. Explosions can chain infinitely. The number of dice equals the sum of a player's figures' attack values in the region, plus any bonuses from Chaos cards.

When a battle die shows a 6, the rolling player scores a hit AND rolls one additional die immediately. If that die also shows a 6, another explosion occurs. There is no limit to the number of explosions in a chain.

A successful battle die result (4, 5, or 6). Each hit must be assigned to an enemy figure or Peasant token in the same region. If hits equal or exceed a figure's defense value, that figure is killed. Different players cannot gang up on the same figure by combining hits.

Some Chaos cards and upgrades grant battle dice to roll at the beginning of the battle phase, before normal dice are rolled. Figures killed by early hits are removed immediately before they can roll their own dice. Early hits combine with the same player's regular hits against tough targets.

An icon appearing on certain Chaos cards (upper right corner) and sometimes imparted to figures by effects. Magic symbols have no direct game effect but are counted for Tzeentch's dial advancement condition and other card effects. A region's magic symbol count includes symbols on Chaos cards played there and on figures present.

Each Power has 5 upgrade cards (2 Chaos Power upgrades and 3 follower upgrades). Upgrades enter play when a Threat dial reveals the Upgrade Card instruction. Follower upgrades replace the matching stats on your Power sheet. Chaos Power upgrades grant new abilities or extra power points.

Each Power has a unique deck of 24 Chaos cards. Cards are played during the summoning phase to card spaces on the board (2 spaces per region). A card's printed cost is spent in power points and also contributes to domination value. Card effects are mandatory if possible. Cards are removed to the discard pile during the end phase.

A shared deck of up to 28 cards (7 used in 4-player, 8 in 3-player). One is drawn each round during the Old World phase. Cards have immediate instructions (italic text, resolved on draw) and some have persistent effects (non-italic, active while on the Old World track). Most manipulate Old World tokens.

A track on the game board with 2 spaces for Old World cards. When a new card is drawn, it goes to space 1, bumping any existing card to space 2. A card bumped from space 2 is removed from the game. Cards on the track can have persistent effects that trigger during the end phase.

An icon on certain Old World cards. Some cards instruct players to remove all Old World cards bearing this icon from the track when drawn. This can disrupt persistent effects that opponents are relying on.

A numbered track on the game board where each player's VP marker tracks their current victory point total. There is no upper limit. If any player reaches 50 VP at the end of a round, the game ends and that player wins (ties broken by highest Threat).

A large reference sheet for each Chaos Power showing: draw phase instructions, dial advancement condition, power point track, follower statistics (summoning cost, attack, defense), round sequence reference, and Old World token guide. Upgrade cards are placed over follower stats when activated.

An Old World token that has no inherent effect. Event tokens mark regions affected by persistent effects of certain Old World cards. If the defining card leaves the track, the tokens remain but have no effect until redefined.

An Old World token that eliminates figures during the end phase. In each region with a Hero token, the player with the greatest Threat who has at least one figure there must remove one figure per Hero token present.

An Old World token that increases a region's Conquest Value by 1 for each Noble present. This does not affect the region's Resistance. Noble tokens are key targets for Slaanesh's dial advancement strategy.

An Old World token with no inherent effect. Peasant tokens serve as battle targets: they take only 1 hit to kill and are removed immediately, placed on the killing player's Power sheet. Some Old World cards award VP for collected Peasants.
An Old World token that decreases a region's Resistance by 1. This makes domination easier but does not affect Conquest Value. Multiple Skaven tokens stack, further reducing Resistance.

An Old World token that counts as the equivalent of one corruption token when determining whether a region is ruined. Also counts toward Tzeentch's dial advancement condition (combined with magic symbols, threshold of 2 or more).
Region 1 in the standard region order. The northernmost region on the map. Not Populous.
Region 2 in the standard region order. Adjacent to Norsca and Kislev. Not Populous.
Region 3 in the standard region order. A Populous region with moderate Resistance/Conquest Value.
Region 4 in the standard region order. A Populous region and one of the most contested areas on the board due to its high Conquest Value.
Region 5 in the standard region order. A Populous region.
Region 6 in the standard region order. Not Populous.
Region 7 in the standard region order. Not Populous.
Region 8 in the standard region order. Not Populous.
Region 9 in the standard region order. The southernmost region on the map. Not Populous.
A region marked with the word 'Populous' on the board. Populous regions tend to have higher Conquest Values and are referenced by certain game effects. Kislev, The Empire, and Bretonnia are Populous.

The standard sequence in which regions are resolved for battle, domination, corruption, and ruination: 1. Norsca, 2. Troll Country, 3. Kislev, 4. The Empire, 5. Bretonnia, 6. Estalia, 7. Tilea, 8. The Border Princes, 9. The Badlands. Earlier resolutions can affect later ones.

Two regions that share a border on the map. Adjacency is relevant for summoning: a new figure must be placed in a region where you have a figure, or in a region adjacent to one where you have a figure.
Phase 1 of each round. Draw one Old World card, carry out its immediate instructions, then place it on the Old World track. The player with the lowest Threat makes any required decisions.

Phase 2 of each round. Each player draws Chaos cards per their Power sheet and resets their power marker to the starting value (plus any upgrade bonuses). All players act simultaneously. No hand size limit.
Phase 3 of each round. Players take turns in power order (cycling) to summon one follower or play one Chaos card, spending power points. Phase ends when all players reach 0 power points.

Phase 4 of each round. Battle is resolved region by region in standard order. In each region, players roll attack dice in power order, assign hits to enemy figures or Peasants, and remove killed figures. Rolls of 4/5/6 are hits; 6s explode.

Phase 5 of each round. Two steps: (1) Domination step — check each region for domination, score Conquest Value VP. (2) Corruption step — place corruption tokens (1 per Cultist), check for ruination at 12+ total corruption.
Phase 6 of each round. Six steps: (1) Remove Chaos cards, (2) Resolve Hero tokens, (3) Resolve Old World card effects, (4) Score ruined regions, (5) Advance Threat dials, (6) Check for game end.

A region with a ruination card on it (face-up or face-down). Ruined regions cannot receive new Chaos cards or Old World tokens. No domination scoring or corruption placement occurs there. Battle still happens and adjacency still applies.

Some effects give a player temporary control of an enemy figure. While controlled, the figure is treated as if it belongs to the controlling player for battle, corruption placement, and other purposes. The figure retains its original statistics, including any upgrades.

When two game effects conflict (one enabling an action and another disabling it), the disabling effect always takes precedence. For example, if one card prevents corruption placement and another grants it, no corruption is placed.

The power point cost to place a follower on the board, shown in a circle on the Power sheet's follower statistics area. Cultists are cheapest (typically 1), Warriors moderate, and Greater Daemons most expensive.

A figure's combat statistic shown to the left of the axe icon on the Power sheet. Determines how many battle dice that figure contributes. Cultists have 0 attack; Warriors and Greater Daemons have higher values.

A figure's durability statistic shown to the left of the shield icon on the Power sheet. A figure is killed when it receives hits equal to or exceeding its defense value. Cultists typically have defense 1; Greater Daemons have the highest defense.

A figure laid on its side to indicate it has been killed in battle. Tipped figures are not removed until all players have finished rolling dice in that region. Tipped figures still contribute their attack values when rolling dice.
A special ability from Slaanesh's Daemonette upgrade card. When upgraded, Daemonettes can only be damaged by battle die results of 6. Results of 4 and 5 cannot be assigned as hits against them.
